mn117:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika's park.
mn117:10.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:10.3: One understands wrong intention as ‘wrong intention,’ and one understands right intention as ‘right intention’: that is one’s right view.
mn117:11.2: Intention of sensual desire, intention of ill will, intention of harming—
mn117:12.2: Right intention too, *bhikkhus*, I say is twofold—
mn117:12.3: there is, *bhikkhus*, right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:12.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:13.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:13.2: Intention of renunciation, intention of non-ill will, intention of non-harming:
mn117:13.3: ‘this, *bhikkhus*, is right intention with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.’
mn117:14.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path?
mn117:14.2: The reasoning, thought [*vitakko*], intention, application, directing, placing of the mind [*cetaso*], and verbal *saṅkhāra* in one whose mind [*citta*] is noble, whose mind [*citta*] is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path and is developing the noble path—
mn117:14.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right intention that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:15.1: One makes effort for the abandoning of wrong intention and for the attaining of right intention: that is one’s right effort.
mn117:15.2: With *sati*, one abandons wrong intention; with *sati*, one attains and dwells in right intention: that is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:15.3: Thus these three *dhammas* run around and revolve around right intention, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:16.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:16.3: One understands wrong speech as ‘wrong speech,’ and one understands right speech as ‘right speech’: that is one’s right view.
mn117:17.2: False speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter—
mn117:18.2: Right speech too, *bhikkhus*, I say is twofold—
mn117:18.3: there is, *bhikkhus*, right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:18.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:19.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:19.2: Abstaining from false speech, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, abstaining from idle chatter—
mn117:19.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right speech with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:2.1: “*Bhikkhus*, I will teach you noble right *samādhi*, with its support and with its requisites.
mn117:2.2: Listen to it, attend carefully, I will speak.”
mn117:20.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path?
mn117:20.2: The avoidance, abstention, refraining, and abstaining from the four kinds of verbal misconduct in one whose mind [*citta*] is noble, whose mind [*citta*] is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path and is developing the noble path—
mn117:20.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right speech that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:21.1: One makes effort for the abandoning of wrong speech and for the attaining of right speech: that is one’s right effort.
mn117:21.2: With *sati*, one abandons wrong speech; with *sati*, one attains and dwells in right speech: that is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:21.3: Thus these three *dhammas* run around and revolve around right speech, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:22.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:22.3: One understands wrong action as ‘wrong action,’ and one understands right action as ‘right action’: that is one’s right view.
mn117:23.2: Killing living beings, taking what is not given, and misconduct in sensual pleasures—
mn117:24.2: Right action too, *bhikkhus*, I say is twofold—
mn117:24.3: there is, *bhikkhus*, right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:24.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:25.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:25.2: Abstaining from killing living beings, abstaining from taking what is not given, abstaining from misconduct in sensual pleasures—
mn117:25.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right action with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:26.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path?
mn117:26.2: The avoidance, abstention, refraining, and abstaining from the three kinds of bodily misconduct in one whose mind [*citta*] is noble, whose mind [*citta*] is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path and is developing the noble path—
mn117:26.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right action that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:27.1: One makes effort for the abandoning of wrong action and for the attaining of right action: that is one’s right effort.
mn117:27.2: With *sati*, one abandons wrong action; with *sati*, one attains and dwells in right action: that is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:27.3: Thus these three *dhammas* run around and revolve around right action, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:28.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:28.3: One understands wrong livelihood as ‘wrong livelihood,’ and one understands right livelihood as ‘right livelihood’: that is one’s right view.
mn117:29.2: Scheming, talking up, hinting, belittling, and pursuing gain with gain—
mn117:3.1: “And what, *bhikkhus*, is noble right *samādhi*, with its support and with its requisites?
mn117:3.2: That is: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, and right *sati*;
mn117:3.3: the singleness of mind [*cittassa ekaggatā*], *bhikkhus*, equipped with these seven factors—
mn117:3.4: this is called noble right *samādhi*, *bhikkhus*, both with its support and with its requisites.
mn117:30.2: Right livelihood too, *bhikkhus*, I say is twofold—
mn117:30.3: there is, *bhikkhus*, right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:30.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:31.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:31.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple, having abandoned wrong livelihood, makes a living by right livelihood—
mn117:31.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right livelihood with *āsavas* [*sāsavo*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:32.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path?
mn117:32.2: The avoidance, abstention, refraining, and abstaining from wrong livelihood in one whose mind [*citta*] is noble, whose mind [*citta*] is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path and is developing the noble path—
mn117:32.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right livelihood that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavo*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:33.1: One makes effort for the abandoning of wrong livelihood and for the attaining of right livelihood: that is one’s right effort.
mn117:33.2: With *sati*, one abandons wrong livelihood; with *sati*, one attains and dwells in right livelihood: that is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:33.3: Thus these three *dhammas* run around and revolve around right livelihood, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
mn117:34.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:34.3: For one of right view, *bhikkhus*, right intention succeeds; for one of right intention, right speech succeeds; for one of right speech, right action succeeds; for one of right action, right livelihood succeeds; for one of right livelihood, right effort succeeds; for one of right effort, right *sati* succeeds; for one of right *sati*, right *samādhi* succeeds; for one of right *samādhi*, right knowledge succeeds; for one of right knowledge, right liberation succeeds.
mn117:34.4: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a trainee is endowed with eight factors, while an *arahant* is endowed with ten factors.
mn117:34.5: There too, by right knowledge, many bad, unskillful *dhammas* vanish, and development [*bhāvanā*] goes to fulfillment.
mn117:35.10: for one of right effort, *bhikkhus*, wrong effort is worn away …
mn117:35.11: for one of right *sati*, *bhikkhus*, wrong *sati* is worn away …
mn117:35.12: for one of right *samādhi*, *bhikkhus*, wrong *samādhi* is worn away …
mn117:35.13: for one of right knowledge, *bhikkhus*, wrong knowledge is worn away …
mn117:35.14: for one rightly liberated, *bhikkhus*, wrong liberation is worn away.
mn117:35.15: And the many bad, unskillful *dhammas* that arise dependent on wrong liberation are worn away for that person.
mn117:35.16: And dependent on right liberation, many skillful *dhammas* go to fulfillment in development [*bhāvanā*].
mn117:35.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:35.3: For one of right view, *bhikkhus*, wrong view is worn away.
mn117:35.4: And the many bad, unskillful *dhammas* that arise dependent on wrong view are worn away for that person.
mn117:35.5: Dependent on right view, many skillful *dhammas* go to fulfillment in development [*bhāvanā*].
mn117:35.6: For one of right intention, *bhikkhus*, wrong intention is worn away …
mn117:35.7: for one of right speech, *bhikkhus*, wrong speech is worn away …
mn117:35.8: for one of right action, *bhikkhus*, wrong action is worn away …
mn117:35.9: for one of right livelihood, *bhikkhus*, wrong livelihood is worn away …
mn117:36.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, there are twenty on the skillful side and twenty on the unskillful side—
mn117:36.2: the great fortyfold exposition of *dhamma* has been set rolling, not to be turned back by any ascetic or brahmin or *devatā* or Māra or Brahmā or anyone in the world.
mn117:37.1: For, *bhikkhus*, any ascetic or brahmin who might think this great fortyfold exposition of *dhamma* should be criticized and rejected would, in this very life, arrive at a blameworthy position through these ten legitimate grounds for criticism and response—
mn117:37.10: if you criticize right knowledge, sir …
mn117:37.11: if you criticize right liberation, sir, then those ascetics and brahmins who have wrong liberation are worthy of your honor, worthy of your praise.
mn117:37.12: Any ascetic or brahmin, *bhikkhus*, who might think this great fortyfold exposition of *dhamma* should be criticized and rejected would, in this very life, arrive at a blameworthy position through these ten legitimate grounds for criticism and response.
mn117:37.2: if you criticize right view, sir, then those ascetics and brahmins who have wrong view are worthy of your honor, worthy of your praise;
mn117:37.3: if you criticize right intention, sir, then those ascetics and brahmins who have wrong intention are worthy of your honor, worthy of your praise;
mn117:37.4: if you criticize right speech, sir …
mn117:37.5: if you criticize right action, sir …
mn117:37.6: if you criticize right livelihood, sir …
mn117:37.7: if you criticize right effort, sir …
mn117:38.1: Even those Okkalas, Vassabhaññas, who taught non-causality, non-action, and nihilism, did not think that the great fortyfold exposition of *dhamma* should be criticized or rejected.
mn117:38.3: For fear of blame, hostility, and reproach.”
mn117:38.5: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn117:38.6: The Great Forty is finished, the seventh.
mn117:4.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does right view come first?
mn117:4.3: One understands wrong view as ‘wrong view,’ and one understands right view as ‘right view’: that is one’s right view.
mn117:5.2: ‘There is nothing given, nothing offered, nothing sacrificed; there is no fruit and result of good and bad *kammas*; there is not this world, there is not another world; there is no mother, no father; there are no spontaneously arisen beings; there are no ascetics and brahmins in the world who are rightly gone, rightly practicing, who, having realized this world and another world for themselves through direct knowledge, make them known’—
mn117:6.2: Right view too, *bhikkhus*, I say is twofold—
mn117:6.3: there is, *bhikkhus*, right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions;
mn117:6.4: there is, *bhikkhus*, right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:7.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions?
mn117:7.2: ‘There is what is given, what is offered, what is sacrificed; there is fruit and result of good and bad *kammas*; there is this world, there is another world; there is mother, father; there are spontaneously arisen beings; there are ascetics and brahmins in the world who are rightly gone, rightly practicing, who, having realized this world and another world for themselves through direct knowledge, make them known’—
mn117:7.3: this, *bhikkhus*, is right view with *āsavas* [*sāsavā*], partaking of merit, ripening in acquisitions.
mn117:8.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path?
mn117:8.2: The discernment [*paññā*], discernment *indriya* [*paññindriyaṁ*], discernment *bala* [*paññābalaṁ*], *dhamma*-investigation *bojjhaṅga* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgo*], and right view as a path factor in one whose mind [*citta*] is noble, whose mind [*citta*] is without *āsavas* [*anāsavacittassa*], who possesses the noble path and is developing the noble path—
mn117:8.3: this is called, *bhikkhus*, right view that is noble, without *āsavas* [*anāsavā*], world-transcending, a factor of the path.
mn117:9.1: One makes effort for the abandoning of wrong view and for the attaining of right view: that is one’s right effort.
mn117:9.2: With *sati*, one abandons wrong view; with *sati*, one attains and dwells in right view: that is one’s right *sati*.
mn117:9.3: Thus these three *dhammas* run around and revolve around right view, that is: right view, right effort, and right *sati*.
